title: Digital Security Act 2018
description: The Digital Security Act 2018 (DSA) is Bangladesh's primary legislation governing cybercrime and digital security, replacing the controversial sections of the Information and Communication Technology (ICT) Act 2006. The DSA establishes the Digital Security Agency, defines digital offences including unauthorized access, digital fraud, and online hate speech, establishes investigative powers and special tribunals for digital crimes, and sets penalties for violations. The Act has been subject to significant controversy regarding its impact on freedom of expression.

provisions.title: Section 1. Short Title, Extent and Commencement
provisions.content: This Act may be called the Digital Security Act, 2018. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. It shall come into force on such date as the Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, appoint. The Act applies to digital offences committed within Bangladesh and, where specified, to offences committed outside Bangladesh by Bangladeshi nationals or against Bangladeshi interests. The Act establishes a comprehensive framework for the prevention and suppression of digital crimes, the protection of digital security, and the investigation and trial of digital offences. The Act is to be read alongside the Information and Communication Technology Act 2006, which it partially amends. The Government shall establish the Digital Security Agency as the central body for implementing the provisions of this Act.

provisions.title: Section 2. Definitions
provisions.content: In this Act: 'computer' means an electronic, magnetic, optical, quantum or other high-speed data processing device or system which performs logical, arithmetic or memory functions by manipulating electronic, magnetic or optical impulses; 'computer source code' means the listing of programmes, computer commands, design and layout and programme analysis of computer resource in any form; 'digital device' means any electronic or digital device including computer, television, telephone, mobile phone, printer, scanner or any other device; 'digital security' means the security of digital devices, digital systems, digital networks and digital information; 'digital crime' means any crime committed through digital devices, digital systems, or digital networks; 'information-communication technology' means the science, technology and engineering of digital data creation, communication, integration, and management, and encompasses computer science, software, hardware, networking, telecommunications, information systems and their applications; 'digital security incident' means any event which poses a threat to the availability, integrity or confidentiality of digital information or the functionality of a digital system.

provisions.title: Section 17. Attacking Digital Devices or Systems of Government
provisions.content: If any person commits or causes to be committed digital attacks on any critical information infrastructure, then such person shall commit an offence under this Act. For this purpose, digital attacks include: unauthorized access to, manipulation of, disruption of, or destruction of any digital device, system or network used by the Government; the introduction of malicious code into government systems; interception of government communications; and tampering with government data. If any person commits such an offence, such person shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years, or with fine not exceeding one crore taka, or with both. The term 'critical information infrastructure' means the digital systems, networks and information resources whose loss, damage or disruption would have significant adverse effects on public order, public health, national security, or the delivery of essential services.

provisions.title: Section 19. Hacking
provisions.content: If any person, without permission of the owner or any other person in charge of a computer, computer system or computer network: (a) accesses or secures access to such computer, computer system or computer network; (b) downloads, copies or extracts any data, computer data base or information from such computer, computer system or computer network; (c) introduces or causes to be introduced any computer contaminant or computer virus into any computer, computer system or computer network; (d) damages or causes to be damaged any computer, computer system or computer network, data, computer data base or any other programme residing in such computer, computer system or computer network; (e) disrupts or causes disruption of any computer, computer system or computer network; (f) denies or causes the denial of access to any person authorised to access any computer, computer system or computer network by any means — such person shall be punishable with imprisonment not exceeding fourteen years, or with fine not exceeding one crore taka, or with both for the first offence, and double the penalty for subsequent offences.

provisions.title: Section 21. Publishing Material Against Liberation War or Founding Father
provisions.content: If any person, through digital media, propagates or broadcasts any information against the Liberation War of Bangladesh, against the spirit of the Liberation War, against the Father of the Nation, against the National Anthem or National Flag, then such person shall commit an offence under this Act. If any person commits such offence for the first time, such person shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, or with fine not exceeding one crore taka, or with both, and for subsequent offences, imprisonment not exceeding fourteen years, or with fine not exceeding two crore taka, or with both. This provision has been subject to significant criticism from civil society organizations and freedom of expression advocates who argue it is used to suppress legitimate political criticism and journalistic activity. The provision applies to content shared through any digital medium including social media, websites, and messaging applications.

provisions.title: Section 25. Sending Offensive or Threatening Messages
provisions.content: If any person, through digital device, sends to any person information in order to inflict annoyance, insult, humiliation, obstruction, threat, intimidation, disrepute or harassment upon such person or causes to spread such information, commits an offence under this Act. If any person commits such offence, such person shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years, or fine not exceeding three lakh taka, or with both for the first offence, and double the penalty for subsequent offences. This provision is intended to address cyberbullying, cyberstalking, and online harassment. However, critics have raised concerns that its broad drafting permits its use against journalists, bloggers, and activists who publish content critical of public figures or government policies. The Digital Security Tribunal shall have jurisdiction to try offences under this section.

provisions.title: Section 26. Collection, Sale of Identity Information
provisions.content: If any person, without permission of the owner, collects, sells, possesses, supplies or uses any person's identity information for deceptive or fraudulent purpose, such person shall commit an offence. If any person commits such an offence, he shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or with fine not exceeding five lakh taka, or with both for a first offence. For subsequent offences, the punishment shall be doubled. 'Identity information' includes: a person's name, date of birth, national identity card number, bank account number, passport number, biometric data, digital signature, and any other information that can be used to identify a person. The collection, storage, or processing of identity information without appropriate security measures shall constitute a factor in aggravating the penalty for offences under this section.

provisions.title: Section 28. Defaming Religious Sentiments
provisions.content: If any person, with an intent to hurt the religious sentiment or values of any person through digital medium, deliberately publishes or disseminates any information, such person shall commit an offence under this Act. If any person commits such offence, such person shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding seven years, or with fine not exceeding ten lakh taka, or with both for the first offence. For subsequent offences, the punishment shall be doubled. This provision has been widely criticized by civil society and international human rights organizations as incompatible with the right to freedom of expression. The broad definition of 'hurting religious sentiment' has been used in practice against persons expressing views on religion that others find offensive. The UN Human Rights Committee has called on Bangladesh to review this provision for compliance with the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights.

provisions.title: Section 33. Digital Security Agency
provisions.content: The Government shall, by notification in the official Gazette, establish a Digital Security Agency (DSA) for the implementation of the purposes of this Act. The Agency shall: (a) prepare, coordinate and implement the digital security strategy of the Government; (b) coordinate digital security measures across all government agencies; (c) establish and operate a national computer emergency response team (CIRT); (d) receive and analyze reports of digital security incidents; (e) issue digital security guidelines and advisories; (f) coordinate with international organizations on digital security matters; (g) advise the Government on digital security legislation and policy. The Agency shall be headed by a Director General appointed by the Government. The Agency shall have the power to conduct investigations of digital security incidents and to require government agencies to implement security measures.

provisions.title: Section 45. Cognizance of Offences
provisions.content: No court shall take cognizance of an offence under this Act except upon: (a) a complaint by the Digital Security Agency or any person authorised by it; or (b) a report by the police officer in charge of a police station. The Government shall establish Digital Security Tribunals for the trial of offences under this Act, which shall consist of Sessions Judges or Additional Sessions Judges. A Digital Security Tribunal shall, as far as practicable, try an offence within 180 days of taking cognizance. If the trial cannot be completed within 180 days, the Tribunal shall record the reasons for the delay and shall complete the trial at the earliest opportunity. Evidence obtained through digital forensic examination shall be admissible in proceedings under this Act if collected and analyzed in accordance with the prescribed procedures.

provisions.title: Section 46. Investigation of Offences
provisions.content: An offence under this Act may be investigated by a police officer not below the rank of Sub-Inspector, who may: (a) enter and search premises upon obtaining a warrant from a court; (b) seize and retain digital devices, computer systems, storage media, and other evidence; (c) require any person to produce digital evidence in their possession; (d) take forensic images of digital devices without removing them from the premises; (e) require service providers to preserve and produce relevant data and records. During an investigation, the police officer may apply to the court for an order requiring a service provider to produce stored communications, traffic data, and subscriber information. Digital forensic examination of seized evidence shall be conducted by trained forensic examiners using internationally recognized methodologies. The chain of custody of digital evidence shall be strictly maintained and documented.

provisions.title: Section 62. Protection of Critical Information Infrastructure
provisions.content: The Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, designate any computer resource, system, or network as a critical information infrastructure if it is involved in the provision of essential services or if its disruption or destruction would have significant consequences for national security, public order, public health, or the national economy. The owners or operators of designated critical information infrastructure shall: implement the minimum security standards prescribed by the Digital Security Agency; report digital security incidents to the Agency within the prescribed timeframe; cooperate with Agency inspections and audits; maintain business continuity plans; and conduct regular security assessments. Attacks on critical information infrastructure shall be treated as an aggravated form of the digital offences defined in this Act and shall attract enhanced penalties. The Agency shall maintain a register of designated critical information infrastructure and shall periodically review the designation.

definitions.definition: Any crime committed through digital devices, digital systems, or digital networks, including unauthorized access, hacking, digital fraud, and online offences defined in this Act.

Digital systems, networks and information resources whose loss, damage or disruption would have significant adverse effects on public order, public health, national security, or the delivery of essential services.
Any event which poses a threat to the availability, integrity or confidentiality of digital information or the functionality of a digital system.
Unauthorized access to or manipulation of a computer, computer system or computer network, including unauthorized downloading, introduction of malicious code, causing damage, or denial of access.
